provisions.title: Leverage Ratio Buffer for G-SIBs
provisions.text: G-SIBs are subject to a leverage ratio buffer of 50% of the G-SIB higher loss absorbency requirement. For example, a G-SIB with a 2% G-SIB buffer would have a leverage ratio buffer of 1%, resulting in a total leverage ratio requirement of 4% (3% minimum + 1% buffer). The leverage ratio buffer must be met with Tier 1 capital. Breach of the leverage ratio buffer triggers distribution constraints through a maximum leverage ratio distribution amount (MLRDA), analogous to the MDA for risk-weighted capital buffers.

provisions.title: Supervisory Treatment of Problem Assets
provisions.text: Supervisors should verify that banks have adequate policies and processes for the early identification and management of problem assets. Problem assets include: non-performing loans (NPLs, typically loans overdue by 90 days or more); forborne exposures (loans where concessions have been granted due to financial difficulty); and watch list exposures. Banks should: classify problem assets using a defined loan classification system (pass, special mention, substandard, doubtful, loss); maintain adequate provisions and write-offs; have workout and restructuring capabilities; and report problem asset data to supervisors.

provisions.title: Valuation in Bank Resolution
provisions.text: Authorities should have powers to conduct valuations for the purposes of informing resolution decisions. Two valuations are typically required: Valuation 1 determines whether the conditions for entry into resolution are met (is the bank failing or likely to fail?); and Valuation 2 informs the resolution actions (what losses need to be allocated, what amount of bail-in is required?). Valuations should be: conducted independently of the bank's management; based on fair and realistic assumptions; completed within a compressed timeframe; and subject to subsequent independent review to determine whether the no-creditor-worse-off (NCWO) safeguard has been met.

provisions.title: SA-CCR: Alpha Factor and Aggregation
provisions.text: The Standardised Approach for Counterparty Credit Risk (SA-CCR) applies an alpha factor of 1.4 to the sum of the replacement cost (RC) and the potential future exposure (PFE): EAD = alpha x (RC + PFE). The PFE is calculated as the product of an aggregation factor and the sum of add-on amounts across five asset classes (interest rate, FX, credit, equity, commodity). Within each asset class, add-on amounts are calculated at the hedging set level with supervisory-specified correlations. Netting benefits are recognised within hedging sets, and partial offsetting is recognised across hedging sets through the aggregation formula.

provisions.title: FRTB Internal Models Approach: Expected Shortfall
provisions.text: Under the FRTB internal models approach (IMA), the primary risk measure is expected shortfall (ES) at a 97.5% confidence level. ES replaces value-at-risk (VaR) as the primary risk metric because it better captures tail risk. ES is calculated as a weighted average of partial ES calculations across different liquidity horizons (10, 20, 40, 60, 120 days) to reflect varying liquidity of risk factors. The IMA also includes a stress calibration period, a non-modellable risk factor (NMRF) add-on, and a default risk charge. Banks must pass desk-level P&L attribution and backtesting tests to use the IMA for each trading desk.

provisions.title: FRTB Trading Desk Structure and P&L Attribution
provisions.text: Under the FRTB, banks must organise their trading activities into well-defined trading desks for the purpose of market risk capital calculation. Each trading desk must pass two tests to use the IMA: the P&L attribution test (comparing the risk-theoretical P&L from the desk's risk model with the hypothetical P&L from revaluation); and the backtesting test (comparing 1-day VaR at the 99th percentile with actual trading outcomes over the preceding 250 business days). Desks that fail these tests must use the standardised approach. This desk-level approval process is a key innovation of the FRTB compared to Basel II.5.

provisions.title: FATF Requirements for DNFBPs
provisions.text: FATF Recommendations apply to designated non-financial businesses and professions (DNFBPs), including: casinos (CDD above specified thresholds); real estate agents (when involved in buying/selling of real property); dealers in precious metals and stones (for cash transactions above specified thresholds); lawyers, notaries, and other independent legal professionals (when preparing or carrying out transactions for clients in specified activities); and trust and company service providers (when providing specified services). DNFBPs should be subject to AML/CFT requirements including CDD, record-keeping, STR, and supervision.

provisions.title: FATF Guidance on Politically Exposed Persons
provisions.text: FATF requires financial institutions to implement enhanced due diligence (EDD) measures for politically exposed persons (PEPs), their family members, and close associates. EDD measures include: obtaining senior management approval before establishing or continuing a business relationship with a PEP; taking reasonable measures to establish the source of wealth and source of funds; and conducting enhanced ongoing monitoring of the business relationship. The definition of PEP covers foreign PEPs (always higher risk), domestic PEPs (risk-based approach), and persons who are or have been entrusted with a prominent function by an international organisation.

provisions.title: FATF Guidance on Non-Profit Organisations
provisions.text: FATF Recommendation 8 requires countries to identify and mitigate the risks of terrorist financing abuse of non-profit organisations (NPOs). Countries should: identify the subset of NPOs that are at risk of TF abuse; apply focused and proportionate measures to at-risk NPOs; conduct outreach to the NPO sector on TF risks; and supervise or monitor at-risk NPOs. Countries should not apply a blanket approach to all NPOs but should focus on those that are at genuine risk. FATF has revised its approach to NPOs to ensure that counter-TF measures do not unduly disrupt or discourage legitimate charitable activities.

provisions.title: FATF Guidance on Trade-Based Money Laundering
provisions.text: FATF has published guidance on trade-based money laundering (TBML). TBML involves the exploitation of international trade transactions to transfer value and obscure the origins of criminal proceeds. Common techniques include: over-invoicing and under-invoicing (misrepresenting the value of goods); multiple invoicing (issuing more than one invoice for the same goods); over-shipment and under-shipment (misrepresenting the quantity of goods); and phantom shipments (fictitious trade transactions). Key AML measures include: customs data analysis; trade finance monitoring by banks; information sharing between customs and financial intelligence units; and international cooperation.

provisions.title: IOSCO Principles for Regulation of Collective Investment Schemes
provisions.text: IOSCO has published principles for the regulation of collective investment schemes (CIS). Key areas include: legal and regulatory framework (authorisation/registration of CIS and their operators); governance (independent oversight, conflicts of interest management); valuation (fair valuation policies, independent verification, dealing with illiquid assets); disclosure (offering documents, periodic reports, ongoing material disclosures); custody (segregation of fund assets, qualified custodians); risk management (investment restrictions, leverage limits, liquidity management); and supervision (risk-based supervision of CIS operators, market conduct requirements).

provisions.title: IOSCO Principles on Short Selling Regulation
provisions.text: IOSCO has published principles on the regulation of short selling. Key elements include: reporting of short positions (to regulators and/or public disclosure above specified thresholds); restrictions on naked short selling (requiring locate or pre-borrow arrangements before selling short); circuit breakers and temporary bans (price-based triggers for restrictions on short selling during market stress); exemptions (for market-making activities, hedging, index arbitrage); and cross-border cooperation (information sharing between regulators on cross-border short positions). IOSCO notes that short selling plays a legitimate role in price discovery and market efficiency.

provisions.title: IOSCO Standards on Insider Trading Regulation
provisions.text: IOSCO member jurisdictions should prohibit insider trading and have effective mechanisms for its detection and enforcement. Key elements include: definition of inside information (material, non-public, price-sensitive information); scope of prohibition (trading, tipping, recommending); persons covered (insiders, persons who received inside information, anyone in possession of inside information); exemptions (legitimate business activities, planned trading programmes, market-making); detection (surveillance systems, unusual trading pattern analysis, reporting obligations); and penalties (administrative, civil, and criminal sanctions that are effective, proportionate, and dissuasive).

provisions.title: G20 Reform of International Financial Institutions
provisions.text: The G20 has called for reform of the international financial institutions (IFIs) to better reflect the changing global economic landscape. Key reform areas include: IMF quota reform (increasing the voting share of emerging market and developing economies); World Bank governance reform (shareholding review, capital adequacy); multilateral development bank (MDB) reform (expanding lending capacity, improving efficiency); and enhancing the voice of developing countries in standard-setting bodies. The G20 has also supported the establishment of new institutions such as the New Development Bank (NDB) and the Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB).

provisions.title: G20 Principles for Quality Infrastructure Investment
provisions.text: The G20 has endorsed the Principles for Quality Infrastructure Investment. The six principles cover: maximising the positive impact of infrastructure to achieve sustainable growth and development; raising economic efficiency in view of life-cycle cost; integrating environmental considerations in infrastructure investments; building resilience against natural disasters and other risks; integrating social considerations in infrastructure investment; and strengthening infrastructure governance. For financial regulation, these principles have implications for the treatment of infrastructure exposures in bank capital requirements, insurance investment regulations, and the development of infrastructure as an asset class.

provisions.title: CPMI-IOSCO Framework for FMI Resilience
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO has published comprehensive guidance on the resilience of financial market infrastructures. Key areas include: financial resilience (credit risk management, liquidity risk management, margin adequacy, default waterfall calibration); operational resilience (business continuity, disaster recovery, cyber resilience, 2-hour recovery time objective); and structural resilience (governance, legal basis, access criteria, transparency). For CCPs specifically, resilience guidance covers: cover-1 or cover-2 requirements for financial resources; anti-procyclicality measures for margin models; skin-in-the-game requirements; and regular stress testing and model validation.

provisions.title: IADI Guidance on Early Detection and Timely Intervention
provisions.text: IADI has published guidance on the role of deposit insurers in early detection and timely intervention for problem banks. Key considerations include: information sharing between deposit insurers and supervisors (access to supervisory data, participation in supervisory meetings); early warning indicators (capital adequacy trends, liquidity stress signals, asset quality deterioration); contingency planning (pre-positioning for potential payouts, identifying potential acquirers for purchase and assumption transactions); and prompt corrective action frameworks (mandatory supervisory actions at defined capital trigger levels). Early intervention can significantly reduce the cost of bank failures to the deposit insurance fund.

provisions.title: IAIS ICS Valuation: Market-Adjusted Approach
provisions.text: The Insurance Capital Standard (ICS) uses a market-adjusted valuation (MAV) approach for the balance sheet. Under MAV: assets are valued at fair value (consistent with IFRS 13); insurance liabilities are valued as the sum of the current estimate (probability-weighted mean of future cash flows, discounted using risk-free yield curves with an illiquidity adjustment) and a margin over current estimate (MOCE). The MOCE represents the compensation a willing buyer would require to assume the obligations. The ICS uses a cost-of-capital approach for MOCE, with a cost-of-capital rate set at 5% above the risk-free rate.

provisions.title: IAIS ICP 19: Conduct of Business
provisions.text: ICP 19 requires supervisors to set requirements for the conduct of insurance business to ensure that customers are treated fairly. Key requirements include: product design and governance (target market assessment, product testing); selling practices (needs-based selling, suitability assessment, product disclosure); claims handling (fair and timely processing, clear communication); complaint handling (accessible, transparent, and fair procedures); and supervision of conduct (monitoring market conduct, thematic reviews, mystery shopping). Supervisors should have the power to impose sanctions for conduct failures and to require remediation for affected customers.

provisions.title: FSB Peer Review Process
provisions.text: The FSB conducts regular peer reviews of its members to promote consistent implementation of agreed international standards. Peer reviews are of two types: thematic reviews (assessing implementation of a specific FSB policy across all member jurisdictions, for example resolution planning, OTC derivatives reforms, or compensation standards); and country reviews (comprehensive assessment of a single jurisdiction's implementation of FSB-agreed policies). Peer reviews include: a self-assessment questionnaire; a review by an expert team drawn from FSB member authorities; on-site visits; and a published report with recommendations. Follow-up reports track progress on recommendations.

provisions.title: FSB Framework for G-SIB Identification
provisions.text: The FSB, together with the Basel Committee, has developed a framework for identifying global systemically important banks (G-SIBs). The assessment methodology uses an indicator-based approach across five categories: size (total exposures, 20% weight); interconnectedness (intra-financial system assets, intra-financial system liabilities, securities outstanding, 20% weight); substitutability/financial institution infrastructure (assets under custody, payment activity, underwriting activity, 20% weight); complexity (notional amount of OTC derivatives, Level 3 assets, trading and AFS securities, 20% weight); and cross-jurisdictional activity (cross-jurisdictional claims, cross-jurisdictional liabilities, 20% weight). Banks above a specified cut-off score are designated as G-SIBs and allocated to buckets with corresponding additional capital requirements (1-3.5% of RWA).
